report:
## Profitability

Coca-Cola (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP) both show revenue growth over the FY2022–FY2025 period, but their profitability trajectories diverge sharply in the most recent year. KO grew revenues from $43.0B in FY2022 [KO:Q0] to $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3], while PEP operates at a much larger scale, expanding from $86.4B [PEP:Q0] to $93.9B [PEP:Q3]. On gross margin, KO's gross profit rose from $25.0B [KO:Q12] to $29.5B [KO:Q15], implying a gross margin of roughly 61.6% in FY2025—meaningfully higher than PEP's $50.9B gross profit [PEP:Q15] on $93.9B in revenue, or about 54.1%, reflecting PEP's heavier exposure to lower-margin food and beverage manufacturing. The most striking divergence is at the bottom line in FY2025: KO's net income surged to $13.1B [KO:Q7] (a net margin of ~27.3%) while PEP's net income fell to $8.2B (a net margin of ~8.8%), a sharp reversal from PEP's $9.6B in FY2024, consistent with FY2025 revenues of $93.9B. Operating income tells a similar story—KO's operating income jumped to $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11] after a dip to $10.0B in FY2024 [KO:Q10], whereas PEP's operating income declined to $11.5B in FY2025 [PEP:Q11] from a peak of $12.9B in FY2024 [PEP:Q10]. Overall, KO demonstrates superior margin quality and a strong FY2025 earnings rebound, while PEP faces margin compression despite its larger revenue base.

**KO:** Coca-Cola delivered a strong profitability rebound in FY2025, with net income reaching $13.1B [KO:Q7] and operating income of $13.8B [KO:Q11] on revenues of $47.9B [KO:Q3], translating to a net margin of approximately 27.3%—the highest in the four-year window shown. Its gross margin of roughly 61.6% [KO:Q15] reflects the premium pricing power of its concentrate-heavy business model, and the FY2025 results represent a meaningful recovery from the operating income dip seen in FY2024 [KO:Q10].

**PEP:** PepsiCo's profitability came under pressure in FY2025, with net income declining to approximately $8.2B from $9.6B in FY2024, and operating income falling to $11.5B [PEP:Q11] from a peak of $12.9B [PEP:Q10], even as revenues grew modestly to $93.9B [PEP:Q3]. While PEP's absolute gross profit of $50.9B [PEP:Q15] dwarfs KO's in dollar terms, its gross and net margins trail KO's considerably, reflecting the cost pressures inherent in its diversified snack and beverage portfolio.

Overall, KO demonstrates superior margin quality and a strong FY2025 earnings rebound, while PEP faces margin compression despite its larger revenue base.

profitability.notes: All key quantitative claims are confirmed by XBRL data. KO FY2022 revenue $43.004B ✅, FY2025 $47.941B ✅; PEP FY2022 $86.392B ✅, FY2025 $93.925B ✅. KO gross profit FY2022 $25.004B ✅, FY2025 $29.544B ✅; implied 61.6% gross margin ✅. PEP gross profit FY2025 $50.859B ✅; implied ~54.2% gross margin (claimed 54.1% — immaterial rounding) ✅. KO net income FY2025 $13.107B ✅, net margin ~27.3% ✅. PEP net income FY2025 $8.240B ✅ (~8.8% margin ✅), FY2024 $9.578B ✅ (rebuttal-defended claim confirmed). KO operating income FY2025 $13.762B ✅ (claimed $13.8B — minor rounding), FY2024 $9.992B ✅ (claimed $10.0B). PEP operating income FY2025 $11.498B ✅ (claimed $11.5B), FY2024 $12.887B ✅ (claimed $12.9B). All figures are well-supported.
missed_evidence: KO FY2024 net income was $10.631B (vs. FY2025 $13.107B), meaning the net income surge (+$2.476B) is smaller than the operating income surge (+$3.770B from $9.992B to $13.762B), implying below-the-line items (taxes, interest, or non-operating gains/losses) partially offset the operating improvement — this nuance is absent from the narrative.

PEP FY2023 operating income was $11.986B, meaning PEP's FY2024 peak of $12.887B was only a one-year high before declining to $11.498B in FY2025 — the analyst's characterization of FY2024 as a 'peak' is accurate but the FY2023 context is omitted.

citation.claim: Coca-Cola (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP) both show revenue growth over the FY2022–FY2025 period, but their profitability trajectories diverge sharply in the most recent year. KO grew revenues from $43.0B in FY2022 [KO:Q0] to $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3], while PEP operates at a much larger scale, expanding from $86.4B [PEP:Q0] to $93.9B [PEP:Q3]. On gross margin, KO's gross profit rose from $25.0B [KO:Q12] to $29.5B [KO:Q15], implying a gross margin of roughly 61.6% in FY2025—meaningfully higher than PEP's $50.9B gross profit [PEP:Q15] on $93.9B in revenue, or about 54.1%, reflecting PEP's heavier exposure to lower-margin food and beverage manufacturing. The most striking divergence is at the bottom line in FY2025: KO's net income surged to $13.1B [KO:Q7] (a net margin of ~27.3%) while PEP's net income fell to $8.2B [PEP:Q7] (a net margin of ~8.8%), a sharp reversal from PEP's $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6]. Operating income tells a similar story—KO's operating income jumped to $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11] after a dip to $10.0B in FY2024 [KO:Q10], whereas PEP's operating income declined to $11.5B in FY2025 [PEP:Q11] from a peak of $12.9B in FY2024 [PEP:Q10]. Overall, KO demonstrates superior margin quality and a strong FY2025 earnings rebound, while PEP faces margin compression despite its larger revenue base.
